**Man Convicted in 1957 Cold Case Released from Prison After New Evidence Emerges**

A 76-year-old man, Jack McCullough, has been released from prison after a judge ordered a new trial in the 1957 cold case of the abduction and murder of 7-year-old Maria Ridulph. McCullough was serving a life sentence for the crime, which was one of the oldest unsolved cases in the US to go to trial.

The case dates back to December 3, 1957, when Maria was playing in the snow with a friend in Sycamore, Illinois. A man approached them, introduced himself as "Johnny," and offered them piggyback rides. When Maria's friend returned, Maria and the man were gone. Her body was found five months later, and despite a massive investigation, no one was ever charged in her murder until 2012, when McCullough was convicted.

However, new evidence has emerged that suggests McCullough may have been wrongly convicted. DeKalb County State's Attorney Richard Schmack conducted a six-month review of the case and concluded that McCullough could not have killed Maria. Schmack found that phone records supported McCullough's alibi, which was that he was in Rockford, Illinois, attempting to enlist in the US Air Force at the time of the abduction.

The new evidence includes a collect call made by McCullough to his parents at 6:57 p.m. from a phone booth in downtown Rockford, which is 40 miles northwest of where Maria was abducted. Schmack also reviewed police reports and hundreds of other documents, including from the Air Force recruitment office, which he said had been improperly barred at trial.

The judge, William P. Brady, ruled that McCullough should be released from prison, citing the new evidence. McCullough's family members were emotional as they hugged and cried after the ruling, while Maria's brother and sister displayed little emotion.

The case has been reopened, and Maria's family remains convinced that McCullough is guilty. They have sought the appointment of a special prosecutor to keep him behind bars. However, the new evidence suggests that McCullough may have been wrongly convicted, and his release from prison is a significant development in this long-standing case.

The case was reopened in 2008, and McCullough was arrested in 2011 and convicted the following year. After an appeal by McCullough, Illinois state's attorney Richard Schmack launched a six-month investigation that found what he calls "clear and convincing evidence" that McCullough is innocent. While the judge Friday vacated McCullough's conviction, he stopped short of declaring him innocent, and a new trial will be held, the Chicago Tribune reports. No physical evidence was ever found to support McCullough's conviction.

**Cigarette Smoke Fuels the Growth of Superbugs: A Growing Concern**

Recent studies have revealed a disturbing link between cigarette smoke and the virulent rampage of superbugs, specifically Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Researchers at the University of California, San Diego School of Medicine have found that cigarette smoke not only harms human respiratory and immune cells but also makes MRSA more resistant to antibiotics and more aggressive in its invasion of human cells.

According to the studies, MRSA exposed to cigarette smoke extract becomes more resistant to the reactive oxygen species and antimicrobial peptides used by the immune system to kill pathogens. This increased resistance allows the bacteria to adhere better to human cells, making it easier for them to invade and cause infections. The study also found that the effects of cigarette smoke on MRSA can last for up to 24 hours, suggesting that the bacteria can inherit this resistance.

The researchers, led by Dr. Laura E. Crotty Alexander, tested the effects of cigarette smoke on MRSA in lab studies using human cells and mice. They found that MRSA exposed to cigarette smoke extract was four times more resistant to macrophage killing than non-exposed MRSA. The study also showed that cigarette smoke-exposed MRSA became more aggressive and better at invading human cells in lab cultures.

In contrast, e-cigarette vapor was found to have a lesser effect on MRSA, making it only slightly more aggressive. The study suggests that cigarette smoke may be an additional contributing factor to the increased susceptibility of smokers to infectious diseases.

The findings of these studies have significant implications for public health, highlighting the need to avoid smoking and reduce the spread of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. As Dr. Crotty Alexander noted, "Cigarette smokers are known to be more susceptible to infectious diseases. Now we have evidence that cigarette smoke-induced resistance in MRSA may be an additional contributing factor."

The study was funded in part by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and was published in the journal Infection and Immunity. The research team hopes that their findings will contribute to a better understanding of the relationship between cigarette smoke and the growth of superbugs, ultimately leading to the development of new strategies to combat antibiotic-resistant infections.
answers: Superbugs such as MRSA are already bad enough. But it turns out that something can make them even nastier: cigarette smoke. Researchers found that antibiotic-resistant bacteria exposed to the smoke became more aggressive and far more difficult to kill, they explain at Eureka Alert. It's almost as if the smoke sets off an alarm that causes the bacteria to strengthen its defenses, explains the Union-Tribune of San Diego. Scientists made the discovery by exposing immune cells to two different batches of MRSA, one normal and one treated with cigarette smoke extract. The bigger the dose of smoke, the bigger the effect, notes Medical Daily. "We already know that smoking cigarettes harms human respiratory and immune cells, and now we've shown that, on the flipside, smoke can also stress out invasive bacteria and make them more aggressive," says lead author Laura Crotty Alexander of UC San Diego. It's a double-whammy: Smokers already have weakened immune systems that make them more susceptible to infectious diseases, and the smoke they inhale seems to make invading pathogens even more dangerous. In this study, the smoke helped MRSA survive longer and kill more mice with pneumonia. (The latest antibiotic-resistant bug to make headlines is a nasty stomach virus.)

The mission, originally known as Solar Probe Plus, will be the first to fly directly into the sun's corona, or outer atmosphere, and will provide unprecedented insights into the sun's behavior and the solar wind. The probe will travel within 4 million miles of the sun's surface, experiencing temperatures of up to 2,500 degrees Fahrenheit and solar radiation intensities 475 times greater than on Earth.

The Parker Solar Probe will study the sun's corona, which is hotter than its surface, and the solar wind, which affects Earth's magnetic field and can cause disruptions to satellites and power supplies. The mission will also help improve forecasting of major space weather events, which can impact satellites, astronauts, and the Earth's power grid and radiation exposure on airline flights.

The probe will be equipped with a 4.5-inch thick carbon-composite solar shield to protect it from the sun's heat and radiation, and will carry a chip with photos of Parker and his revolutionary paper on solar wind. The mission's objectives include tracing the flow of energy that heats and accelerates the sun's corona and solar wind, determining the structure and dynamics of the plasma and magnetic fields at the sources of the solar wind, and exploring mechanisms that accelerate and transport energetic particles.

The Parker Solar Probe will make 24 close flybys of the sun, some of which will bring it within 3.7 million miles of the sun's surface. The mission is scheduled to launch in July 2018 and will take seven years to complete. The findings from the mission will help answer questions about how stars work and improve space weather forecasting.

The mission is a testament to the importance of Parker's body of work, which founded a new field of science and inspired many important science questions that NASA continues to study and further understand. Parker, who is 89 years old, was honored with the NASA Distinguished Public Service Medal and a scale model of the probe.

It will provide unprecedented insights into the sun's corona, solar wind, and the mechanisms that accelerate and transport energetic particles. The mission will also help improve forecasting of major space weather events and provide a better understanding of the sun's impact on Earth's magnetic field and radiation exposure.

answers: NASA is going to the sun. More specifically, it's launching an unmanned probe next year that will travel closer to the star than any spacecraft has done previously. Eventually, it will be whizzing around the sun at a speed of 450,000 miles per hour, reports CNN. The mission ends in 2025. Corona: The probe will actually fly into the outermost part of the sun's atmosphere, called the corona, per a mission overview at NASA that touts "humanity's first visit to a star." The heat: The probe will rely on a 4.5-inch-thick carbon-composite solar shield to help it withstand temperatures up to 2,550 degrees Fahrenheit, reports Orlando's WKMG. Parker, who turns 90 in June, is a professor emeritus at the University of Chicago, and the Guardian recounts that the theory he put forward in 1958 about a stream of charged particles flowing from the sun was once thought to be "crazy." A first: This is the first time NASA has named a mission after a living scientist. "I'm certainly greatly honored," said the man himself, per Space.com. "The more we know about how these processes work, the better we can get at predicting when they will happen," writes Loren Grush at the Verge.

**Holiday Season Data Breaches: Neiman Marcus and Target Affected**

The holiday season has been marred by a series of high-profile data breaches at major retailers, with Neiman Marcus and Target being the latest victims. According to reports, Neiman Marcus's computer network was penetrated by hackers as far back as July, with the breach not being fully contained until January 1. The company disclosed the data theft of customer information late last week, stating that some customers' payment cards were used fraudulently after making purchases at their stores. Neiman Marcus has apologized for the breach and has offered one free year of credit monitoring to affected customers.

In a similar incident, Target Corp. suffered a massive data breach that compromised 40 million credit- and debit-card accounts, as well as personal information belonging to 70 million people. A 17-page report from computer firm iSight details the type of software used in the hack, which was caused by a type of malware placed in store point-of-sale systems. The report notes that the malware was effective and was used to steal credit and debit card data from tens of millions of customers.

Investigations into both breaches are ongoing, with a joint investigation by state attorneys general examining whether the two attacks are related. Both Neiman Marcus and Target have denied any connection between the breaches. The report from iSight suggests that the malware used in the Target breach was created in regions known for their software coding prowess, including Eastern Europe and Brazil.

The report also notes that the tactic used during the Target holiday hackers is "new to eCrime" and "covertly subverts network controls and common forensic tactics to conceal all data transfers." This type of hacking is expected to continue, as it offers big rewards for cyber crooks with little risk.

The holiday season data breaches have raised concerns about the security of consumer data and the need for retailers to take more proactive measures to protect their customers' information.
answers: It looks like the hackers that hit Target had lots of other, well, targets. Yesterday, iSight released its own report, saying that a virus it's calling KAPTOXA "has potentially infected a large number of retail information systems"—and noting that the "intrusion operators displayed innovation and a high degree of skill," particularly in terms of the "operational sophistication" of the hack. The Journal shares this feature by way of example: The virus focused on stealing data during the peak hours of 10am and 5pm; the data was housed in a Target server that the hackers later accessed. The virus attacks point-of-sale systems in a way that is "new to eCrime," subverting traditional efforts to protect consumer data, the report warns, according to NBC. Parts of the code, which is impervious to all known antivirus software, have been online since last spring. It's partly in Russian, which US officials think may indicate a link to organized crime in the former Soviet Union. The finding follows reports of similar breaches at Neiman Marcus. That breach had gone unnoticed since July, the New York Times reports today; Neiman's system was only fully secured Sunday.

**Archaeologists Uncover 6th Century Relic of John the Apostle in Bulgaria**

In a groundbreaking discovery, archaeologists at the Burgas Regional Museum of History in Bulgaria have unearthed a 6th century lead vessel containing ashes from the alleged grave of John the Apostle, one of Jesus' twelve apostles. The relic was found in a reliquary at a medieval fortress site in Burgas, Bulgaria, which dates back to the 6th century C.E.

The reliquary, measuring less than an inch long, is decorated with crosses and is believed to have been brought back from the grave of John the Apostle in Ephesus, Turkey, by a pilgrim from the fortress of Poros. According to local legend, pilgrims would visit the grave of John the Apostle on May 8, the date of his death, and collect a holy powder, known as manna, which was believed to have healing properties.

The discovery is significant not only because of its historical importance but also because it is one of only 43 known lead tubes from the 6th century, a time when pilgrimage to the Holy Lands was common among Christians. The relic is also unique in that it features images identical to those found on clay tube reliquaries produced in ancient Ephesus.

The Burgas fortress and port, where the relic was found, has a rich history dating back to the Late Antiquity period, with evidence of Roman, Byzantine, and Bulgarian empires having occupied the site. The fortress was also the site of a major battle in 1304 AD, where the Bulgarian Tsar Theodore Svetoslav defeated the Byzantine Emperor Michael IX Palaiologos.

The discovery of the relic is considered one of the most important in the history of the Burgas Regional Museum of History, and it sheds new light on the history of early Christianity and the significance of pilgrimage sites in the 6th century.
answers: Archaeologists in Bulgaria think they've found ashes belonging to one of the 12 apostles. The team found a small lead container with ashes while excavating a basilica that dates back to the sixth century AD, reports Archaeology in Bulgaria. The markings on the inch-long vessel match those at the grave of St. John the Apostle in what is now Turkey, leading researchers to surmise that a long-ago Christian from Bulgaria traveled to the grave site and returned home with the sample of ashes. Such journeys were common around that time, reports Novinite. “Probably a pilgrim from the Foros Peninsula went on a pilgrimage to Ephesus, and came back here with this relic, which was then donated to the basilica on Foros,” says the director of the Burgas Regional Museum of History in Bulgaria. (The ancient basilica is located in what is now the Black Sea city of Burgas.) Archeologists also found a Bulgarian royal seal from the 10th century at the dig site, which includes a fortress, notes the Week. (Another archaeological discovery: an ancient underground city in Turkey.)

Recent archaeological discoveries in Kenya's Olorgesailie Basin have shed new light on the evolution of human behavior and technology. Excavations at the site have revealed that early humans, likely Homo sapiens or their close relatives, developed advanced toolmaking skills and trading networks around 320,000 years ago. This is significantly earlier than previously thought, challenging the long-held view that modern human behaviors such as symbolism, innovation, and art emerged around 40,000-50,000 years ago.

The findings also include evidence of the earliest known use of pigments, with bright red ochre discovered at the site displaying signs of grinding and cut marks. This suggests that early humans used pigments symbolically, possibly for personal adornment or to mark territory.

The discoveries at Olorgesailie challenge the traditional view of human evolution, which held that modern human behaviors emerged outside of Africa. Instead, the evidence suggests that complex behaviors and technological innovations arose in Africa, where early humans adapted to changing environments and developed new tools and social networks.

The findings have significant implications for our understanding of human evolution and the emergence of modern human behavior. They suggest that the "human revolution" that made us modern may not have been a sudden event, but rather a gradual process that occurred over hundreds of thousands of years. The discoveries also highlight the importance of considering the complex and nuanced history of human evolution, rather than relying on simplistic narratives of revolution and progress.

The research team, led by paleoanthropologist Rick Potts, suggests that the early humans of the Middle Stone Age were able to adapt to changing environments and develop new technologies through social networking and information sharing. This ability to cooperate and innovate allowed them to survive and thrive in a challenging environment, and laid the foundation for the development of modern human societies.

Overall, the discoveries at Olorgesailie provide a new perspective on human evolution and the emergence of modern human behavior. They highlight the importance of considering the complex and nuanced history of human evolution, and suggest that the development of modern human societies was a gradual process that occurred over hundreds of thousands of years.

Researchers previously believed the Early Stone Age marked by large, primitive tools (hand axes and cleavers) made way for the Middle Stone Age and its smaller tools (spearpoints and blades) around 280,000 years ago. But stone tools found in Kenya's Olorgesailie Basin show hominin groups—it's not clear which species is responsible—had developed these more advanced tools approximately 320,000 years ago. "We see a smaller technology, a more diverse series of stone tools … designed for specific purposes," researcher Rick Potts tells NPR. An expert not involved in the study says the tools are so "prepared and retouched" they suggest the Middle Stone Age likely started even earlier. What drove it? Microscopic and chemical analyses of the region's soil show the landscape was rapidly shifting between wet and dry conditions, meaning hominins might have been forced to adapt. But as the technology also coincides with the emergence of Homo sapiens in Africa, it's possible that the human mind deserves credit. The tools themselves suggest the development of trading networks, too. Some were made of rocks carried from 55 miles away, says Potts. Others were made of obsidian from as far as 30 miles away, per the Conversation. (These stone tools are also making waves.)

The decision comes after a 14-year effort led by Chancellor Nicholas S. Zeppos, who stated that the name was a "symbol and a reminder of racism, slavery, and a very, very bloody Civil War." The university had previously attempted to rename the building in 2002, but was blocked by a court ruling. However, with the help of anonymous donors who pledged $1.2 million, Vanderbilt was able to meet the condition and remove the name "Confederate" from the building. The university's decision is part of a broader effort to address diversity and inclusion on campus, including the addition of a chief diversity officer to the leadership team.

The move is not an isolated incident, as universities across the country are grappling with the legacy of the Confederacy and its symbols. Middle Tennessee State University is also seeking to remove the name of Confederate Gen. Nathan Bedford Forrest from one of its buildings, but faces a tougher hurdle due to a new Tennessee law that requires a two-thirds majority vote to approve changes to historical markers.

The decision to rename Confederate Memorial Hall has been met with both praise and criticism. Ariana Fowler, president of Vanderbilt Student Government, praised the university's leadership for listening to student concerns and taking a step towards creating a more inclusive environment. However, Doug Jones, an attorney who represented the Daughters of the Confederacy, called the move "a real slam on the history of our country" and argued that rewriting history is "terrible."

The renaming of Confederate Memorial Hall is a significant step towards acknowledging the painful legacy of the Confederacy and its impact on marginalized communities. As universities across the country continue to grapple with their complex histories, Vanderbilt's decision serves as a model for how institutions can take responsibility for their past and work towards a more inclusive future.

Chancellor Nicholas S. Zeppos called the inscription on Confederate Memorial Hall "a reminder of racism, slavery and a very, very bloody Civil War." The Nashville university's efforts to change the name in 2002 were halted when the United Daughters of the Confederacy sued. The group’s $50,000 donation in 1933 helped build the dorm. A state appeals court ruled the building could be renamed Memorial Hall only after Vanderbilt gave the United Daughters back their money—$1.2 million in today’s dollars. The university has the cash, thanks to a raft of anonymous contributors who wanted the tie to America’s painful past broken for good. "It's a symbol that is, for many people, deeply offensive and painful," Zeppos told the Tennessean. "And to walk past it or to have to live in that space is really something that I just don't think is acceptable.” Yet tampering with Confederate imagery remains controversial, and a new state law makes it harder to do so, the AP reports. Efforts by Middle Tennessee State University in Murfreesboro to remove the name of Confederate Gen. Nathan Bedford Forrest from a building could face a tougher climb under the Tennessee Heritage Protection Act, which now requires a vote of two-thirds of the state’s Historical Commission rather than a simple majority.

The researchers hope to expand their model to include more visual modules, which would improve the resolution and accuracy of the reconstructed videos.

While the technology is still in its infancy, it has the potential to revolutionize various fields, including medicine and law enforcement. For instance, it could enable people with paralysis to control their environment by imagining sequences of movements, and potentially even allow for the decoding of memories and dreams. However, the accuracy and reliability of the technology are still uncertain, and its potential applications in detective work or court cases are limited by the nature of memories and the technology itself.

The researchers acknowledge that the technology is not yet ready for widespread use, but it marks a significant milestone in the field of neuroscience and has the potential to unlock new insights into the workings of the human brain.
answers: Scientists at UC Berkeley have made a major advancement in the field of mind reading, reconstructing YouTube videos based on brain scans from people who’d seen them. Researchers would put subjects into an MRI machine and track their brain activity as they viewed videos. Once they’d build a model of how a subject’s mind processed the video, “we could read brain activity for that subject and run it backward … to try to uncover what the viewer saw,” one study coauthor tells ABC News. **Rare Celestial Event: Super Blue Blood Moon Total Lunar Eclipse on January 31, 2018**

A rare celestial event is set to occur on January 31, 2018, when a total lunar eclipse, also known as a "Blue Moon," will take place. This event is even more special as it coincides with a "Supermoon," which occurs when the moon is at its closest point to Earth in its orbit. The eclipse will be visible from eastern Asia, across the Pacific to western North America, and will be the first total eclipse of a Blue Moon in nearly 152 years.

**What is a Blue Moon?**
A Blue Moon occurs when there are two full moons in a single month, which happens about once every 2.7 years. The second full moon in a month is called a Blue Moon.

**What is a Supermoon?**
A Supermoon is a full moon that occurs when the moon is at its closest point to Earth in its orbit, making it appear 14% bigger and 30% brighter than a regular full moon.

**When and Where to See the Eclipse**
The total lunar eclipse will begin at 11:48am UTC on January 31 and reach its maximum at 1:30pm UTC. Viewers in eastern North America will need to wake up early to catch the partial eclipse. The eclipse will be visible from eastern Asia, across the Pacific to western North America.

**How to See the Eclipse**
The eclipse is safe to view with the naked eye, and there is no need for special glasses. However, if it's cloudy or too cold to go outside, you can watch the event live on the Virtual Telescope project's livestream.

**Photographing the Eclipse**
To take a great photo of the eclipse, use a smartphone or DSLR camera and adjust the exposure settings to capture the right balance of light. Consider putting the moon in context with a local landmark to give your photo a sense of place.

**Additional Events**
The Virtual Telescope project will also be hosting a livestream of the Supermoon rise over Rome's skyline on January 1, 2018, and the lunar eclipse from Australia on January 31, 2018.

**Don't Miss This Rare Event!**
Mark your calendars for January 31, 2018, and get ready to witness this rare celestial event.
answers: Look up at the sky on Jan. 31 and you may witness a sight unseen for 150 years. For the first time since 1866, a total lunar eclipse will occur with the blue moon, or second full moon of the month, which—like the one visible on New Year's Day—will also be a supermoon. Got all that? When the moon is at or near the closest point to Earth in its orbit (aka, a supermoon), it appears 14% bigger and 30% brighter than full moons that occur at the farthest point in the moon's orbit, though the moon will lose brightness as it enters Earth's shadow, according to NASA. Because of the way Earth's atmosphere bends light, the eclipsed moon will take on a reddish hue, something often referred to as a blood moon, making this rare celestial event "a super blue blood moon eclipse," according to the Miami Herald. The next blue moon eclipse won't occur until 2028. The eclipse will last for 3.5 hours, with the total eclipse stretching for 77 minutes, reports Sky & Telescope. Those along the Pacific Rim from Alaska and northwestern Canada to central and eastern Asia, Indonesia, New Zealand, and Australia will get the best view of totality during the middle of the night. However, partial phases of the eclipse should be visible under a clear sky in central North America, western Asia, India, the Middle East and eastern Europe, per Space.com. North American viewers should look up as the moon sets on the morning of Jan. 31, while those on the opposite side of the world should see a partial eclipse as the moon rises. Not in an ideal viewing area? The Virtual Telescope plans to livestream the total eclipse from Australia, which you can watch here, per Quartz. (Bogus glasses damaged a solar eclipse viewer's eyes.)

The country is holding its first parliamentary elections since Mubarak's departure, with the final round of voting taking place in nine provinces, including the North and South Sinai governorates. Islamist groups, particularly the Muslim Brotherhood's Freedom and Justice party, are expected to consolidate their gains from earlier rounds, with some analysts predicting they may win a clear majority of the seats in the new parliament.

The Brotherhood has played down its electoral strength, but winning a majority would enable the party to govern without forming a coalition, potentially reducing the influence of ultraconservative Islamists and liberal parties. The Brotherhood has stated its intention to form a coalition or unity government, but winning a majority would give it more flexibility in its decision-making.

Meanwhile, Mubarak is on trial for corruption and complicity in the killing of over 800 protesters during the uprising that led to his ousting. The chief prosecutor, Mustafa Suleiman, accused Mubarak of imposing "tyrannical rule" and devoting the last decade of his rule to ensuring his son, Gamal, would succeed him. Suleiman described Mubarak's regime as corrupt and said the former president's lust for power would forever tarnish his legacy.

The trial has been marred by procedural delays, including a demand by lawyers representing the victims that the presiding judge be removed. Mubarak faces the possibility of the death penalty if convicted of complicity in the killing of protesters. His two sons, Alaa and Gamal, are also on trial, facing corruption charges.

The elections and Mubarak's trial are taking place amid growing opposition to the ruling generals, who have said they will not cede power before presidential elections are held by the end of June. The new parliament is expected to take authority over the hiring and firing of a prime minister, but the military rulers have said they intend to retain that authority.

The country is also facing economic challenges, with growth slowing due to unrest and a decline in foreign-currency reserves. The benchmark EGX 30 stock index has lost 49 percent in value last year, making it the third-worst performer among world markets tracked by Bloomberg.

answers: The final round of voting began in Egypt today, and it looks like the Muslim Brotherhood could walk off with a controlling majority of seats in Parliament's lower house. The Muslim Brotherhood's Freedom and Justice Party, the country's mainstream Islamist party, had already won a high percentage of the seats awarded in the first and second rounds of voting. Two of the nine provinces voting today are Brotherhood strongholds, where some of the party's most well-known candidates are running, the New York Times reports. A plurality, not a majority, was originally predicted for the Freedom and Justice Party. A majority would allow the party to govern alone, but the Brotherhood has repeatedly said it plans to form a coalition government, and has aligned itself with more liberal parties rather than the ultraconservative Salafis, who are in second place with up to 25% of the seats so far. Also today, prosecutors began presenting their case against former Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, Bloomberg notes. His trial resumed last week after a two-month break; state TV today showed Mubarak being carried to and from the courtroom on a gurney. "He deserves an end of humiliation and indignity," said the chief prosecutor, according to the AP.

pred:
**Car Suspended from Telephone Pole after Driver Reacts to GPS Directions**

A bizarre incident occurred in Mendon on Wednesday evening when a Toyota Corolla became suspended from a telephone pole's guy wire after its driver, Nabila Altahan, reacted too quickly to a GPS system's instructions. According to police, Altahan was driving westbound on Route 4 when her GPS device suddenly directed her to turn around, as she had passed her intended destination. In a swift reaction, Altahan left the road, and her car ended up dangling from the telephone pole's support wire.

Fortunately, neither Altahan nor her passenger was injured in the incident. Witnesses, including a driver who happened to be passing by, reported that Altahan jumped out of the car as it was suspended from the pole. The State Police and Rutland City Fire Department responded to the scene.

This unusual incident serves as a reminder of the importance of carefully following GPS directions and not reacting impulsively to sudden changes in navigation instructions.
answers: Police in Vermont say a car ended up almost vertical when the driver swerved quickly in response to her GPS ordering her to "turn around." The car was suspended almost vertically on guide wires attached to a utility pole (see a photo here) in Mendon on Wednesday night, reports the AP. Police say 30-year-old Nabila Altahan of Dorchester, Mass., was headed west on US Route 4 when she passed her intended destination and reacted quickly to the instructions, leaving the road at a significant enough speed to propel the vehicle up the wires. She wasn't injured. (This driver followed her GPS into a lake.)
